[User AndrewNC](https://rateyourmusic.com/~AndrewNC "User AndrewNC") [AndrewNC](https://rateyourmusic.com/~AndrewNC)[May 22 2008](https://rateyourmusic.com/music-review/AndrewNC/slowdive/souvlaki-4/15619483)▼![5.00 stars](https://cdn.sonemic.net/2.5/img/images/10m.png)

A holy pillar on the hill that stretches skyward as if to touch the sun when in the evening the sun meets the tip of the pillar and the illusion of the weight of the sun being held up by a man made artifact makes all question our power to invent; man creates god so god can create man. However the sun is sacred and there will always exist that in which we will never understand.

Slowdive’s Souvlaki stands on it’s own. It needs not to be compared to other music that is not Slowdive as it stands on it’s own has not been matched by any of their contemporaries (if they had any) or any of the bands they influenced. So, why write a review? Closure, basically. If I review this, one of the most influential albums on the development of my musical tastes, I will finally feel at ease with my RYM identity. Or something.

The album begins with a bang. Instant hazy, dream bank of guitars laden with feedback and great drum work to keep this wealth of codeine induced haze afloat. All gates open. The lyrics set the mood right away conjuring up images of floating, TV-covered walls, etc. A space-pop shoe gazing song about a girl whose life is in shambles, drugs taking over and Neil trying to get through to her but she just laughs, casually flicking her hair out of her eyes with the back of her hand, laughing. Instead of getting through to her it seems the narrator is content to go along for the ride down the spiral stairs. As much as he wants to get out and knows he should her “messed up life still thrills \[him\]”. Like the two songs that follow, this song fades and it seems all too premature as it’s just about 3:00 of song before it slowly fades out. What a tease, but it kind of fades like Alison’s life is flickering out of sight. Effective!

_I guess she’s out there somewhere_

Like every Slowdive song, it’s simple lyrically. But there’s nary another pop song out there that brings forth so many images and conflicted emotions. One of the most beautiful recorded songs I’ve heard in this life.

Druggy verses with light acoustic flourishes fighting their way to be heard in the mix, Machine Gun is a much more melancholy fare. A couple years ago I casually likened the song to one of those dreams in which one is drowning, which a lot of us have at some point in life. Although, instead of being a grey, cold death, the song seems like the ray of light hitting the water surface and splitting into a hundred dancing trails of possibilities. Even to a non-believer such as myself.... heavenly is the only word to describe the sensation felt on this one.

40 Days is like Slowdive's stab at a driving rock song. It's still typically Slowdive, but unlike anything on prior effort [Just for a Day](https://rateyourmusic.com/release/album/slowdive/just-for-a-day/), it isn't slow. Neil’s voice is surprisingly upfront in the mix and the band

delivers the goods on the latter half with whaling guitars when Neil retreats into the shrouded noise.

The atmosphere is calm again suddenly. Some kind of otherworldly rainforest, walking on the forest floor whilst looking up at the layers. Dripping water and now dusk is setting in. Rachel Goswell’s amazing voice enters the fold. She possesses that ghostly, breathy voice displayed on Ballad of Sister Sue. I find it truly amazing the contrast between the haunting voice that whispers a sweet death and the angelic, higher register she possesses and on this particular track we are treated to both in unison. This gives away to delicate Here She Comes with Halstead lamenting

_“It's so lonely in this place,_

_so cold I don't believe_

_and theres no one knows my name_

_It's easy to pretend_

_It's easy to believe_

_There's a shadow on my wall_

_It dances like my soul”_

So simple, yet so great. Instead of giving way to more mellow fare, the band press forth with their most experimental production, the somehow aptly titled Souvlaki Space Station. Teasing us at the start with some minor, spacey feedback and some soft cymbal embellishments. Anyways, the percussion slowly builds up and reverb-laden guitars grow louder and louder, passing through your ears and spiralling space bound. Again, Rachel’s vocals are majestic. A dove soaring amidst the chaos. Frenzied, jazzy, peaceful and spacey all at once, this truly stands as one of my favourite recorded pieces of music of all time. Impossible to follow up, but When the Sun Hits does anyways. Their only other song that I’d call close to ‘pop’, the dualist vocals are a prime example as to why Mojave 3 succeeded instead of being just another dreary alt. country band. R&N’s voices meld together brilliantly and the playful (“What? Playful?! Slowdive?”) way they sing “hey, hey, lover” gets me every time. It just does. Anyways, the song chugs along beautifully. Then comes the dubby, comedown from the high of Altogether; a lot of people’s dismissive views on this track are those in which I shall never understand. I’m not sure if Neil was on codeine when he recorded this, but it works. Like being put in a pod full of thick smoke for three hours, looking at snapshots of your life played in quick blips. All the most effecting moments… discoveries, hardships, all played chronologically.

Then the album gets really sad. Melon Yellow doesn’t reinvent the wheel for the group (unfortunately it gets a lot of unwarranredslack for this too, by people with ADD that seem to think the album dips in quality here… fuck ‘em) but the chorus is pretty heart-wrenching…

**Limerence** refers to an involuntary cognitive and emotional state of intense romantic desire for another person…Can one overcome this power? It’s a miserable thing to ponder while listening, but it’s damn pretty.

Then comes Dagger as if the heart strings weren’t being pulled enough. A sparse close to a layered record, it’s about a couple who are slowly wounding each other emotionally and the realization that they can’t stay together or they’ll be in more pain. He’s hurting the “sunshine girl” he loves as he is too sad to feel the pain, but knows he’s causing her a great deal. The world is full of noise and when your alone, silence is deafening.

_I didn't really lose you_

_I just lost it for a while_

Tragic. The perfect finish to a damn beautiful, sad record that came from a band at the height of their powers, even though their own story was tragic at this point. Being dumped by the UK press in favour of an American trend that rolled in on the waves called grunge. Soon the 'dive were funding their own tours out of their torn pockets and after this record, they committed commercial suicide with a polarizing album that was Neil’s artistic statement as well as his resignation from the record company and the band’s swan song. But that’s for another time. The band should be congratulated for making their grand artistic statements no matter how not-commercial they were at the time.

It's just perfect...

perfect lyrics, perfect tunes, perfect cover art. It's the kind of record that makes you think about your impossible love and the way you suffer about it. When you feel like shit, slowdive's Souvlaki always understands you and knows the way you feel.

[User shutupandbreakdance](https://rateyourmusic.com/~shutupandbreakdance "User shutupandbreakdance") [shutupandbreakdance](https://rateyourmusic.com/~shutupandbreakdance)[Jun 05 2008](https://rateyourmusic.com/music-review/shutupandbreakdance/slowdive/souvlaki/11831123)![2.50 stars](https://cdn.sonemic.net/2.5/img/images/5m.png)

I must say something: shoegaze, to me, is the equivalent of really slow, really corny new wave music in early 1990, when new wave was all but phased out. I will always view it as a bunch of kids who never liked punk, but for some reason or other wanted to be associated with every aspect of its culture. I don't hear heartbreaking emotion; I hear Boy George crying all over again for an altogether different reason (my point being, who cared to begin with?). In short, shoegaze is new wave's bastard in a basket, _not_ Post-Rock's surrogate mother (Talk Talk's _Spirit Of Eden_ can much more accurately be credited as such).

Nevertheless, I'll give Slowdive's so-called masterpiece _Souvlaki_ a formal review. "Alison" favors a spacey, droning ambience right from the get-go, though it's obvious Slowdive prefers their pop influences over their shoegaze influences, as the stereotypical "wall" of ambience resides at just the right level, just behind the driving beat and melodic vocal line. My Bloody Valentine's _Loveless_ set the standard for what would forever be voted upon as shoegaze, and Slowdive added a little more straightforward pop sensibility to that equation. "Machine Gun" borrows the chorus and phase-drenched synth-swells from every Phil Collins tragedy, but redeems itself to some degree with Goswell's pretty vocal harmonizing. "40 Days" is "Alison" a second time around. "Sing" proves once and for all that Slowdive does, in fact, possess creative talents all their own, with its drippy keys and skipping beat, and of course Goswell's haunting vocals. I'm reminded of Slowdive's followers King Black Acid, and even Radiohead's "Climbing Up The Walls" off _OK Computer_. "Here She Comes" is a fairly lovely, breathy-tremolo thing. "Souvlaki Space Station" sees the bass cranked up a bit, the guitars a little further out in space, and the vocals in some neighboring galaxy - a very good track. It conjures up the emotions I felt during my very first listen to _Ágætis Byrjun_, and what do you know? Now we know where "Karma Police" got its ending (boo). "When The Sun Hits" is more late eighties regurgitation in a new decade of what should have been fresh insurgency (Shrimp Boat's _Cavale_ took on the task, also paving the way for The Sea and Cake and all their musical triumphs to come). "Altogether" and "Melon Yellow" really ruin the album's clout thus far, as we are once again ushered back into the land of bad The Church and Echo-esque dirges. "Dagger" is that painful moment when your friend sits you down and explains how he's written his ticket to fame, in just under four minutes. Give a kid a guitar, and he'll take more than just your money and your time - he'll kidnap an entire generation's better sense. Pity.

I'll be honest I'm not the biggest fan of my bloody valentine. I know, I know loveless is supposed to be one of the highlights of the 90s. Not to mention people say they're the first link to the evolution of garbage (with curve being the middle link). There's one song on loveless that demonstrates this and that's soon (the last song on loveless) which is actually really good. My main problem with loveless though (even though it is a bit of a grower) is all the droning guitar noises. The dream pop vocals were decent but they really didnt do too much for me. I figured all shoegaze was like this but then I heard about this album by slowdive that got a 4.07 and anything that gets a 4 or above, to me, is worth checking out so I did, and...

I loved it! there were a couple songs that were so so but otherwise it was awesome. One reason why it's more awesome is that there's more and better dream pop vocals and less droning guitars. There is some but it's more quiet. The other reason is that it seems like more of an ancerstor of garbage's that MBV. Not to mention it just plain sounds better and if you ask me the guitarists and drummer are better too. One song that particularly blew my mind was dagger. I never thought that shoegaze could be performed using just a acoustic guitar (with no droning electric guitars in the background whatsoever). That song was fucking incredible

the highlights for me are: the opener alison. you can never go wrong with a girl's name for a title and this one is one of the best songs of the 90s. Another one that definitly earned a spot on my ipod is sing. For the record i dont put whole albums on my ipod because I dont have space for them so I just put the highlights. Plus of all the songs on this album sing reminds me of garbage the most. And the best of all is souklavi space station which you should definitly check out.

Altogether this album renewed my interest in shoegaze and I think I'll definitly have to try some more in the future.

"Listen close and don't be stoned"

This is just... ahn... happiness, freedom, nostalgia, peace, distortion and delay. It's all that everybody loves about the 90s. It's so beautiful you just want your life to be like it. Souvlaki makes me think "man, I wish I were a teen in a bedroom with a window on the second floor that could be reached by climbing a tree. By the way, where is my pot-smoker shoegazer girl when I need her?"

[User yerblues](https://rateyourmusic.com/~yerblues "User yerblues") [yerblues](https://rateyourmusic.com/~yerblues)[Sep 03 2012](https://rateyourmusic.com/music-review/yerblues/slowdive/souvlaki/517743)![3.50 stars](https://cdn.sonemic.net/2.5/img/images/7m.png)

Contrary to popular opinion, _Souvlaki_ has always been my least favorite Slowdive album. Don't take this statement too seriously though. It's like saying you have a least favorite Ferrari. No matter what, that car is still gonna be pretty awesome. _Souvlaki_ is more accessible than either of their other full-length albums, and does bolster two of their best tracks in "When the Sun Hits" and the nearly instrumental "Souvlaki Space Station," which features that gooey, opiate haze that epitomizes their sound at its best. Brian Eno makes contributions to two songs, "Sing" and "Here She Comes." Unexpectedly, they end up being two of the weaker tracks here. One of the reasons that I sour on this album a bit is the sequencing. With the exception of the Eno-related tracks, the first seven songs constitute the most impressive run of songs in the Slowdive catalog. After that, however, things come to a grinding halt, leaving the last ten minutes of the album to be relatively ineffective. As a result, the ethereal effect of the album does not quite match _Just for a Day_ and it lacks the experimental challenge taken up by _Pygmalion_. All things considered, though, the record proves why Slowdive was the best thing in shoegaze not named My Bloody Valentine.
